I'm on page 96. I am at the close. I Show you this page only because I like the third quote so well a lot of people don't even know zig had a brother who worked as a sales trainer and We we called judge zig's evil twin They looked alike they sounded alike, but kind of like the Duke twins There's a reference for you Judge was kind of the opposite of zig behavior only so judge Five pack a day smoker drink while he was working Hardcore direct sales guy so Zig would never have said it, but judge says you can't wipe your ass with him. Maybe it takes money to buy toilet paper. I like the quote. It's pretty cool. So Here we are finally at closing And There's different kinds of closes so there were a lot of questions and again Steve there were a lot of people who asked your question So who's this are all the way from kangaroo land So it would be great if you could talk about lead generation strategies from the stage We're going to do road shows selling consulting service in the room to lead gen form to interview then closing meeting sort of like the financial Advisor model I got a version of the question from a lot So there's obviously different things we're going to close on with a presentation One is all we're going for is an appointment a next-step commitment. So we'll talk about that first So I am on your page 97 by the way So for lead generation Responses in a room or on a tele-seminar on a webinar Often It pretty much only requires a very clear instruction So specific to the financial advisors for example a lot of times the reason they wind up with this few Appointments is because they weren't very clear Now we're going to get your appointments booked Here's the forms Susan's back there Take your form to Susan and Susan's going to set your appointments sometime in the next four days and Give you the short list of things. It would be ever so nice if you brought it with you to your appointment often they're not that direct and explicit and So, you know, I said yesterday one of the things to keep in mind about all of this Is one of the things people are pretty good at? Generally speaking is following direct instructions. They're very good at standing in line They all great at that I'm not but most people are really good at it. I'm amazed when I go places and say You know, they're gonna stand this monkeys concert I mean, there's a million people in line to get in and they're being bossed around and they're in lines And I'm thinking you would think people would mutiny at this, you know, I Just went through somebody in a slot machine and waited until it was one minute before time to start There was no line then you went in, you know, but they're in line for 30 minutes. Why they got a reserve seat Where's it going to go? Get it, but so most people are pretty good about following explicit instructions. So often the failure is just We didn't give him explicit instructions, right? second alternate choice is usually better than single choice so It's the appointment with choice of date and time it might be the appointment or If you're not yet ready to schedule an appointment We want to get you booked for a diagnostic call on the phone with one of our coaches And so you want to go get under the blue flag with your form, right all in a choice You have to decide how much information you're going to request in this environment Obviously the more information you request the bigger the impediment to Scheduling a meeting the less information the less the impediment. So that's a faucet you have to decide What you know if your goal in life is? The most number of appointments out of the room or off the webcast is to diagnostic calls It's humanly possible and you've got a bunch of salespeople who need to be fed and They can get blood out of a rock and you just to say that's one thing if you only got like one good salesperson Or you're doing it and you only want to be on the phone or in front of a super qualified prospect Then you got to be okay with driving 80% of them away and not having them register in order to get the ones who do Register to fill out a two-page form say something interesting We actually are closing not lead generating but at the current version of the Phil Grove get rich in real estate weekend seminar There's three or four different things sold But then the last thing sold is the hundred thousand dollar two-year big dog thing And they actually go in and meet one to one and somebody closes them so Early results only three cities, but we've nearly doubled the closing percentage by forcing them to fill out a four-page questionnaire To go in the room and sit down with the closer Interesting You got to decide on reassurances versus reasons not to So you will see you know the no salesman will call copy has been around since there was a phone So you got to decide again, you don't want to make a promise you won't keep But you know if you come in and meet with us, we will never pursue you We will never chase you. We won't don't follow up with you What we our discussion either makes sense to you and you ask how to go forward So you got to decide whether you want to bet that reassuring and ultimately you got to deal with procrastination versus urgency So you got to think about how can we get them to do this? Right now, right and there's either got to be scarcity. There's got to be exclusivity There's got to be danger of loss from waiting There's got to be a bribe that they want the one thing I will tell you even when all you are doing in a physical room Is booking an appointment it is better to have some thing That they walk out of there with then just an appointment slip Just as you will sell more product if you have the product piled up You will book more appointments if you have something piled up that they get to take home with them well In your situation where you really are B2B and you are serious I would make it more a relevant useful gift than I would a toy gift Yeah, a tool of reference but not something they have to get through before the appointment In some other environments, yeah, it's the nice gift It's the you know, we got a limited number of these I'm a patriot Ronald Reagan coffee mugs and so the first 10 old people who come back and get their appointment taken care of with their financial Advisor you get this cup and you get a bib with a flag on it and you get you know, you know and you get a hurricane I mean, there's nothing wrong with that either, right? So If you look at your page 98 This is This is a piece out of the video sales letter not the one you saw This is a piece out of the video sales letter for the free preview So the clothes here is is there this is so we're still in the lead generation kind of game They're registering to come to the free preview. There is a gift It is a relevant gift then The other thing you need to know about driving them to appointments one They need to know what to expect at the appointment So again to use your example the financial advisor one of the reasons they muck it up And they don't get as many appointments as they think they should is because they sort of leave it vague What's going to happen when you come to the appointment and nobody likes uncertainty? And they will generally imagine something more onerous and worse and difficult Than it is so they need to know exactly what's going to happen to them And what won't happen to them when they come to the appointment or the diagnostic call Which is usually the alternative if we're selling this at a distance that you want them to do Secondly, they should be aware of some benefits that occur By the call or the appointment that occur whether or not they ever go any further than that or not Because they're giving up time So it'd be cool if even if I don't like you and I don't want to do business with you after we meet or after I talked to you on the phone I learned something I Know something I didn't know before That I can do something with independently Alternate choices again, we talked about that How much info to request urgency procrastination clear marching orders, okay, so for purchase on 101 It's not that different We want a clearly understood offer So A lot of group presentations fall apart because people don't they're just so confused About the offer itself. They don't know is it a seminar is it a home study course? Is it a pill is it surgery? What the hell is it? So we want a good clearly understood offer and Any environment in which you can do it have a good order form Albert's rule for print was you ought to spend as much time on the order form as you spend on the sales letter If you can control it at the back of the room not only do you want a really good order form, but you want a really good crew There's the biggest Worst thing about the Peter Low events is we had bad crews So you're driving them back there and you've got terrible people at well He would think from mediocre to terrible it varied, but you didn't have good Tommy Hopkins flew five in All right So he put five of his people there on top of everybody else's people just to sort of shove them out of the way and do it I mean that basically was what was going on so a good order form that restates The offer the components the value build if you relied heavily on value build Irresistible offers of course better the offer Once you finally get to it to better the results you're going to get So irresistible offers are all about the structure of price to value Are we going to show price or we're going to show individual price items that add up to some malarkey number That is then deeply discounted with some reason why J. Douglas Edwards called it the reduction to the ridiculous You can sometimes do it by individual pricing sometimes you could do it by apples to oranges comparison You know here's a box with ten seminars in it And if you came to all ten seminars you would have spent thirty six thousand four hundred and twelve dollars plus airfare in hotel rooms And the whole thing is four hundred ninety nine dollars now. We didn't compare apples to apples. We compared apples to price versus desire What's the value of the desire so we can really talk about what will it be worth to you? You know MasterCard has this priceless campaign they run. It's the most successful ad campaign. They've ever run You know the value of this is priceless right gift with purchase and toy in box So gift with purchase almost always helps an offer because people like getting something for nothing If they intellectually know there's something for nothing it doesn't make any difference Everybody is stimulated by the idea of getting something for nothing So gift with purchase almost all we should be in an offer okay, the toy in a box so So if you're if you're my age You grew up not only with the toy in the cracker jock box you grew up with toys in the cereal box most cereals completely made out of sugar That children might eat had toys in them Sometimes they weren't very good toys, but they were toys and the cereal company put them at the bottom of the box so you either ate it or Dumped it out into like I only did that once You dumped it out into eight bowls and then put them back in the cupboard in order to get to the tie this This the toy this was a this is a system. I only used one time But the toy in the cereal box is the thing they want more than anything else So when you create the bonus the gift with purchase that is the one thing that they really want So like if I If I'm selling the entire magnetic marketing system to dentists Chiropractors pick a group, but we'll use dentists. I mean it took time You know, but it drives a lot of sales so Irresistible offer risk reversal of course Great offers have great risk reversal the more of the sense of risk you take away The more you can drive the sale My overarching advice about that is Don't make guarantees that you're going to be grumpy about honoring Be willing to honor them even when the person is wrong But then make the boldest best guarantee that you possibly can and understand Guarantees risk reversal is all math Right, none of it is emotion thus you cannot be emotional about it. It's just Math, that's all it is Does it lift sales enough? To more than justify its expense if it does end of story now How wrong Susan is when she? You know wants her refund and how late she is and how she only sent half the stuff back and We can find her with Susan at eBay having made 56 copy none of that matters Right, it's math. That's all it is. But the more you make risk go away Obviously and if in your category you think There's no risk sense of risk you're wrong Whatever it is you do people are frightened of you And they are frightened of themselves So they either sense risk in doing business with you or they sense risk in their ability to then Do use benefit from the thing that you are selling there I was in There's a store in Amish country From here if you're ever here with time on your hands, it's about an hour around 15 minute drive. I mean the Peter Lowe sales thing at the back was horrible If you drive them to phones and you aren't controlling the phones if you so the ease of buying Starts with who they have to deal with besides you what they have to do to get an order placed I Was the only one by the way at the success events the entire nine years. We're not doing any that crap quick quick quick quick simple here And I always thought step as long as you get me a phone number You can screw up everything else the address can be wrong. You could be dyslexic. You can spell the name wrong I don't give a shit we can fix it. Just get me a phone number get their bag get them out of the way So the next person can buy because there's 300 people staying in the line And they wanted to go home an hour and a half ago the ones at the back of the line are walking away I did a seminar Many years ago for a buddy of Jim Newman's I can't think of his name right now Richard Souton so Jim Newman wrote the book release your breaks if you don't know Nelson I told Nelson this I did a church marketing seminar for pastors You can laugh. I said this is hilarious You know, I give them all the credit I give them all the credit in the world At I was the only one at the Peter Low events. You don't see it on our order forms now You did see it on mine during recession years I'll do it now with a big ticket item. You'll still see it occasionally, but I learned it from a customer I didn't know then occurred to me and it should have because I watched my mother do this for years so Customer comes to the table. I happened to overhear it. Can I it wasn't Peter Lowe that it was years before that Can I split this purchase on three credit cards? And of course the sales prevention department says no I Go yes Come with me Now what exactly well I know exactly how much I got on this one and I know how much I got available on this one And I don't have enough on any one of them, but if I use all three of them great I got it good here. We'll figure this out right and then I started putting it on order forms So here you can tell us credit card charge this much to it credit card charge this much to it credit card Charge this much to it did it all through the success track years you'll be amazed how many dentists split a $399 purchase into put this much on this credit card this much on this credit card should have occurred to me How much my mother do it for years little notes on the back of all the credit card keeping track of how much was left and available credit You know like 25 credit cards and there's $18 on this one and there's $42 on this one and she wasn't alone, right? so But you know the corporate answer to that is 56 reasons why we can't do that You know we can only have one credit card for one purchase and we have to swipe it then we so beware of the sales Prevention activity that gets in your way when you are selling to people over and over again If you can just say we have your credit card on file just say yes, right? Why why not do that right terms? so There's a whole issue about Whether you want to do installment payments whether you want to do to pay whether you want to do three-pay in some cases you can Hide it so you can get as many sales as you can in one pay And then you can go back to everybody that didn't buy who were at the presentation or who watched the webcast or who won the Tele-Seminar and you can then give them the to pay the three pay the four pay the 52 pay option and in other cases where you can't People fear the trade down They fear if you do if I say to you you can do it in three-pay now everybody will do it three-pay This is a false fear now. It will vary by the audience. I will grant you that But my experience is if I do three-pay and I don't do any incentive even to Pre-pay a huge number of people pre-pay They still do it one-pay a They don't want to screw around with installment payments. The money item is not that big to them The kind of person they are is that they don't buy on installments And most of the time the trade in increased revenue is worth far more than the hassle and even what little trade down occurs So if you think your number is a significant number to the people you are selling to not a prohibitive barrier number Just a significant number So different significant number for you for me for Trump for you know But there's some number at which we for some thing we go If it's that number you may want to do to pay or three-pay You also want to think about price structure if you do an AB offers where you do the gambit where the amounts the same Just the number of payments so the basic offer is Two payments of 200 the deluxe offer is for payments of 200 the person is still they're just making a $200 Choice right all you've done is extend the number of payments Overall the important thing is make buying as easy as possible when you are selling group whether Online or in a room make it as easy as you possibly can for them to get the transaction done And if it's big numbers as fast as they possibly can so see if they even just wander off to the restroom They may not come back if they don't get taken care of pretty quick now somebody's trying to talk them out of it You know, it's why in most size groups if I'm selling from the stage. I want order forms passed out I don't want them going to the back to get an order form because Everything moves faster if they have an opportunity to fill it out at the table while I'm finishing the pitch and while they're moving Urgency People will wait So that's believed scarcity key word here is believed You have to find ways to make it believable and if you sell to people repetitively via group presentations or really by any other media You have to act in a way that does not have them stop believing you so believed scarcity believed exclusivity Believe price change if they wait believe deadlines a reason why for the above So you can't say you can only have it if you do it right now And you could never have it if you don't do it right now if you don't have a reason for that Otherwise you won't be believed or you'll just be thought of as a mean nasty, you know cruel so big The decisive action argument or other status argument so if you can make acting now a Self-esteem reinforcing thing and you can make not acting now The equivalent of going and standing under the white flag with a big loser on your forehead You will get more people to act now So you can do a rap one kind of rap about another about the power of decisive action Foster because he talked about Napoleon Hill a lot and all the success track seminars He used the whole Andrew Carnegie Napoleon Hill unbeknownst to Napoleon Hill Andrew Carnegie had a stopwatch under the desk, which I don't think they had stopwatches and that doesn't make any difference All right, you had a stopwatch under the desk and he gave Napoleon Hill only 60 seconds to say yes or no To his offer and after pulling hill and not been a decisive individual History itself would be changed and you would not have read a copy of thinking grow rich And we would never know the 17 laws of success and the same thing is true about you being able So he would do this whole rap about Andrew Carnegie in the stopwatch. I have no idea if it's real or not I don't know. I don't care. It doesn't make any difference to me But there's you could build a rap about that makes being decisive a Self-esteem affirming act So you want to put that in there and you can use of course impending events by buying this You are coming to this you are benefiting from this and it's happening tomorrow next week today When we group sell area exclusive programs at the end of the discovery day fast start days tomorrow So you buy now you're going to the victory dinner and then you're doing fast start tomorrow You don't buy now you're going home And you're not going to dinner out So that's loser winner and it's impending event I only gave you one reference for clothes, which is Zig's book. You really don't need any others Having them is actually quite irrelevant You have again two samples to look at a Current video sales letter this one arthritis pain goop and the night at pieces of the 1999 script that match up again. You will see structurally Not much difference. So let's leap to page 120 where we will deal with some technical stuff in the last 15 minutes in a In a physical environment One you pretty much want to block easy exit with product tables Ideally you want them in the room not outside the room if they're outside the room They're set up in a way that doesn't make it easy to get to the John's or get out the door of the hotel You don't want to let them stick it all the way down in their bookstore on the second floor 500 miles away from the room that you're in it makes a difference All right, it's that's the reason why you can't get off the Disney attraction without walking through the gift shop Casino laid out is so you got a snake to get to a restroom past slot machines and gaming and bars You can't just walk right to the restroom from wherever you are There is never a direct path to a restroom in a casino from any place. You can't get there All right, they didn't do that because they were drunk when they designed a place They did it because go in or common they got a chance of you stopping and Dumping some money in a machine. So where you put the sales activity? matters If you are in a big event like the low events where I was one of the great virtues of being last Because we would have eight product sales areas up on the second floor of the arena on the ground of the arena There's a mess most of the time I could get them to shut them all down and come to one on the floor During the time I was on stage because I was last so I could pipe pipe or everybody to one place If there are multiple locations use you to draw everybody as much as possible to one place So yep, you saw them all day long folks. They're still there. There's six places. You can go get this stuff I'll be at that one Okay, come see me and we'll talk Strong expectations conveyed again tell people what the hell they're supposed to do Don't be shy when you get to the close Here's what's gonna happen now I will almost always if I'm gonna hang around somewhere in a physical environment I'm happy to stay here and answer questions with you talk to you as long as you would like but go take care of business first Get your stuff then come see me Mostly they'll go do that The ones you don't they won't buy period so be rude to them and get them out of the way before the people had bought get there You want a stampede? Physical stampede causes people to join the stampede. You've got to get them moving You just have to if they're not moving. It's difficult to close their sales I saw foster do it once his most amazing thing. I've ever seen I would never have had them I would today I would never balls to do it. I mean I It's about 25 docks in a room. He turns them loose. They're supposed to come back and get their stuff They got forms. He's done the I'm filling the form out now with my pen and my American Express card You take out you don't just all these people have done nothing and they don't get up at the break. They sit there I'm in the back. I've never seen it never seen it before haven't seen a sense nobody moves he marches slowly Graciously Gentile to the back gets a bundle of stuff Marches all the way back up to the front down to the furthest guy at that table and puts it in front of them Walks goes all the way back. I'm serious 30 times Puts it back. It's another puts it down. Everybody's watching him. Nobody's moving I'm standing in back thinking damnedest thing I ever saw. I don't know where it came from I don't know I have no idea it'll be really interesting to see what happens, you know, it's probably not going to be good But it'll be interesting. So he does this 30 times takes forever And finally walks to the back. It stands there 100% one after another Filled the form out came back handed in the fall nobody said a damn word they went to the John They came back they sat down they all have to stop I ever saw anything like it But usually Stampede they joined the stampede. So you got to think through how am I going to cause a stampede scarcity with the gift with Purchase we only have this number of this only the first 35 people are going to get the tail calls Are the you got you need to stay a pee you will always sell more because people will join the stampede They don't like to be the dummy sitting there while everybody else is running second bite at the apple Structure your sales opportunity your speaking opportunity if you can so that you come back After the selling break now, I never had the opportunity to do that a little events But in most other environments and the ones I built you could do that We have it for Phil now with the real so the main sales break when people go back and get their stuff You want them coming back to the seats with the stuff? So now you're here and you got stuff and you're looking at it And you're taking it out of the bag and you know and he's got stuff and a little annoying guy He ain't got no stuff And he's like trying to look at their stuff and you know He kind of gets the message and then now in your next segment you can just be dumb So you can speak and act as if everybody's got the stuff Right so now take out your green thing and turn to page 37 and right now. We're going to talk about what's on page 37 But he can't do that because he ain't got no page 37 All right, and it's even better if it's something they get to take apart You know take out that thing and pull the red tab and the purple thing will fall off and in there will be the so They're all pulling out their shit. He ain't got no shit You'd be amazed how many people at the end trudge back and get stuff because they've been embarrassed and they're disappointed And they're frustrated and if you structure it where there's multiple breaks So it's another break and then you're coming back to do more stuff a bunch of them who didn't get stuff Go get stuff because they got to come back and sit there again with no stuff So they either leave or they get stuff So it forces purchase of stuff a sumptive language all During the pitch live webcasts You are also striving for pressure You don't have as easy an opportunity to create it right So you still need it so the buy now button The operators are standing by now you see it in direct response TV sometimes Where they show the call center You'll see it the DR TV spot. You'll see it in an infomercial So they don't just say operators are standing by they show you a bunch of operators The reason they in a telethon the reason they got the people on stage on camera answer the phones All right, they could have been in another room somewhere and have more stage for the elephants of the dancing girls but it's important to show you the people who are answering the phones and By the and the dumb ones have them sit there with no phone activity instead of coaching them if There's no call coming in pick up the phone and talk for then put it down, you know, it's okay You get to be an actress You know I mean so I'm understanding by strong expectation Conveyed verbally and visually on a webcast. We still need scarcity of something So again one of the things you'll see us do if you watch GKICs again and again and again Is you will see the new or additional? additional gift with purchase added segment by segment by segment Now we play fair So everybody who bought at commercial break number one Well, we come back towards the next commercial break We add another something And we give it to everybody who bought already So it stacks so nobody's penalized for being an early buyer, but every segment. There's another thing Added on right because who's left people who are resisting buying so let's pile on some more You can have as I talked about earlier. You can have quasi shills You know like if you're going to do a webcast and you are doing it to your own base You want to take extra care to make sure some people are on the webcast to buy everything? So there will be after the first break you'll be able to say and Susan from New York And you're thank you Susan and congratulations and Charlie and you know them all by name The first ones those are quasi shills. They're not fakes. They're real buyers, but you made sure they were there Recognition of buyers by name Countdown of the clock. Those are some of the things you can do with the live webcast applying to both Here are the presenters tells That the audience reacts to More subconsciously than consciously so the first tell that That communicates to the audience or something wrong here. You probably really shouldn't buy this Etc is is the presenter who's Has a physiological change as he moves from What he thinks his content to what he thinks is commercial when I got him Foster was the worst I've ever seen in my life Absolutely the worst I see it a lot, but he was the worst the meeting planner guys running it actually I was working for Tracy's uncle and Foster was on before me a group of chiropractors and I said to Rodney at the back of the room I said he's the best speaker. I've ever seen in my life Which still today that's true and I said he's the worst salesperson platform salesperson I have ever seen a fire hydrant could do a better job than this how the hell does because I know Rodney paying him I say he's getting what he sells. I said how could he possibly make a living and he wasn't I mean so he did content Steve content content content content content and he's a tall kind of elegant Gentleman-looking guy nice Perfect good voice kind of an old classic orator voice You'll hear him if you were here for wealth on the CDs. I gave you as a gift. So now it's time Call to action. Here's Foster's call to action the script's horrible, but as he does it He liked shrinks And I mean he's sort of in a begging position his voice drops a couple octaves no eye contact He's now looking out into space. I mean it's horrible horrible if the pitch had been any longer than the one I'm gonna gotta give you he would be like in the Wizard of Oz, you know, they just eventually there's a pool Lay in there and he's gone. I mean the wicked witch gets dissolved and there's nothing there But a green glob of stuff. Here's his pitch some people ask me if They can have some of my material to take home with them There's some cassette programs in that suitcase at the back of the room Just take what you want and put in what you think it's worth This is his pitch I'm like, oh my I fixed this guy by the way. I want you I fixed this guy To a 95 to 99 percent close rate Evening after evening after evening it wasn't easy But I see people do it all the time they like Not that radical, but they physiologically shift As they are about to ask for money And even if the audience doesn't consciously like get it they subconsciously get it And see if you're not okay with it has the seller. I Know I'm not supposed to be okay with it has the buyer So I don't buy next tell any hint of apology Now you can with the right audience in the right place you can be cute You can let him in a little bit, but you cannot be apologetic Again, I see speakers do it all time They'll actually apologize You know so now I'm only going to take ten minutes of your time and you know I know you came here to learn and I know but I need to tell you about Well, what have you just told everybody? What I'm doing here now is horrible. It's abusive. It's criminal. I shouldn't be doing it But buy from me anyway No, I don't think so I don't think I'm going to do that self Disparaging you were when shifting into the selling it has the same effect as the apology Now if it's in the story before the bridge, it's okay If it's after the bridge, it's not okay Because people get that it's a cover mechanism for embarrassment and shame about what you're doing Disrespectful handling of the physical product if you are going to handle it if I'm selling you this I can't do That when I'm done with it and walk back to the projector I've seen it Okay, or any variation of that I have to handle it lovingly When I display it to you and I have to nicely put it back and make sure it's okay Before I move on to the next one. So disrespectful handling of product confusion If you are not going to memorize anything for any presentation and There it may that may be fine by the way memorize the pitch Because if you're uncomfortable with it at all that's the place you're suddenly good to look like an Alzheimer's victim And it's it's good to be Evident you sort of have that deer in the highlight hot headlights look and you're fumbling and oh I forgot I meant to tell you about this you are destroying the thing and in many instances in addition to pitching You should like we do in the webcast cut to a pre canned perfect CTA That now it can't Be embarrassed or ashamed or forgetful or confused because you scripted it you voiced it You videoed it so it is perfect And no reason not to have a playing in a live event either if you're on a break in there coming back last hurrying the pitch So people who have so he said I was picking on him. I guess I am so the Steve Miller question right of the We have this boundary between content and Selling here's what those people tend to do They suck up all the time on the content and they leave themselves almost no time for the pitch And then they race through the pitch The CTA itself they race through it So number one what does that do well people don't know what the hell they're buying The most important part of the presentation is that That's why you did the rest of it Was to get the opportunity to sell the stuff So if you're going to short anything don't short that Secondly it telegraphs to people again. You don't feel very good about this See he's trying to get through this as quick as possible And get the hell out of here So now they sense that and they don't feel very good about that And if by the way you have promised the 17 laws of and you somehow are running out of time just skip some But don't put yourself at a position to hurry the pitch Whatever time it's supposed to take delivered perfectly you take That time now if you're in a multi-speaker environment you cannot abuse the person behind you If you do it in our environment and I have an opportunity I will fix it So you will never work in our world again So if you are supposed to be done at 1010 in a multi-speaker environment You should be done at 1010 so you got to take it out of the front. You can't just keep going Until you actually get your 22 minutes of CTA Okay, horribly unprofessional. That's not the answer So format changes throughout a presentation So there is and I'm mostly talking now about video and online There is direct-to-camera monologue Person talks to camera Direct to camera, but with an audience so you do need to understand If you watch PBS and you watch the pitch shows on PBS You will see people make this mistake a lot So you will see them do what I am doing now So you will see them Kind of walking back and forth and making eye contact with people in the audience and all that However, you aren't in the audience. You're at home watching it on TV So we're doing TV even though there's a live audience Therefore your job is to work to the camera Now you can do a little bit of this, but you can't do much of it I didn't get it. I was saved at the Peter Loew events by a speaker running my Pat Frep early So there's 10,000 people in here. First of all with lights and stuff. I can only see the first two rows Then it's just a blur All right, and I'm doing what I would do if I had 200 people in the room. I'm working the room You know, and I'm trying to see is he with me Can I monkey with him? She want eye contact. That's what I'm doing And after one of the early ones I was in San Francisco Airport and packing over said you were great There's one thing you don't know about what you're doing. That's a great. Tell me what I don't know She says everybody but the first two rows not only can't you see them? They can't see you. They're watching the jumbo trunks You're on television. You're not on stage Therefore the camera So you don't take the product do this you do this you're on Camera you're doing TV and unfortunately you're doing a one-camera shoot Said I got it and I watch people Flounder and fail in that environment often because they didn't know what I now knew because she told me you could usually Improve somebody's results the minute you fixed that I didn't volunteer it to everybody I only volunteered it when I was getting paid by somebody, but I'm not as kind as Pat But you automatically improve their results when you fix that right because most of people were watching them on a big TV so Monologue direct-to-camera with or without an audience one-to-one interview you can do you You're the pitch person whoever that is and a host one-to-one interview be very careful. You don't see this is no good because you sink No good, right the seats right now on the Who show is it it's Kimmel the seats on Kimmel show are horrendous I mean people sink into them and little guys like Tom Cruise just there's a head I mean You know it's like it's like you see in Florida, you know where cars go past you There's nothing there, but a head and two little hands on a steering wheel, you know There's nobody else there. They're small and they're low So you those are no good, right? So you got to make sure if you do interviews you and usually most people their energy level dips When they are Comfortably seated so the best interviews are like on these if you're going to do them Even at a live event if you have somebody who's if you decide to do this If you have somebody one of your events who has something great to sell It's of great value, but they can't sell with a shit And maybe they can't even speak with shit Then you can do an interview on stage and you can carry that now if you do too much of it then you negate Your ability to sell anything, but so the one to panel interview Okay, you can do that in the environment obviously you can do the PowerPoints presentation. I Will caution you about this in a live environment One of the quickest ways to suck energy out of the room is to turn on the PowerPoints Everybody's experience with PowerPoint speakers most of them are horrible The better the quicker way to drain the energy out of the room is go to PowerPoints presentation and dim the lights That'll do it That'll do it They say here comes the college professor Oh Now I'm not good enough to work without help But you will I'll die before you'll see me doing PowerPoints It just ain't gonna happen And I will tell you did mark use PowerPoints Mark use PowerPoints right which did not help him stay on track at all by the way, so It's its primary purpose didn't even didn't even deliver itself, right? So he and I have had this discussion for years The day he switched to PowerPoints is selling effectiveness on stage drop by half And it's never come back if I could wean him off PowerPoints he'd sell twice as much stuff Physical demonstration in the studio Physical demonstration in the field so now you have to show it on video Okay, and you can cut the video on a monitor. Just don't go there too long You see we student a lot of infomercials now you would now you can do with Skype till you can cut the Skype Just don't do it too long Because they will decide they don't want to pay attention length and breakdown of presentations I said an infomercial is 28 and a half minutes the DVD sent to the Homer office can be considerably longer As long as you don't bore them and all of that I will mostly do 40 45 to 90 minute Presentations if I'm going to get somebody to request the DVD and send them a DVD online the infomercial length all the way up to an hour is about right the people who tell you seven minutes They're full of crap It has an example when you break it down so an infomercial 28 and a half Fairly typical format is you got three segments You're good at you really only have 21 and a half minutes left after you take out the CTAs the first CTAs the longest second CTAs shorter than the first the last CTA is the shortest of everything and so you got to break down your timing your time Schedule of any of these presentations and then figure out how you apportion it You don't see this is not like a sales letter So when you write copy for a sales letter my approach is I write until I'm done right, I'm being Whatever it takes if it's 28 pages fine if it's 36 pages fine if it's 52 pages fine It's right until I take well here. We have finite time so you got to create a time frame of Your acts your sections your CTAs and then you got to fit things into them webcasts you can obviously do three or four hours We're doing them very successfully Some people are doing longer Again you've got to break out your CTAs now you have X amount of time left What are you going to do? Are you going to do three segments or you get a four segments? You got to create a schematic that you're going to fill in the boxes preview seminars my preference is three hours My own preference and experience is much more than three You shouldn't need now. I know guys are doing all day, but I think it's excessive and we used For success track and then later for Jay Geier I'm trying to take fill down to three So in three hours you have the ability to have a first break where nobody sells anything a Second break where all the sales is done and they come back and you still have a shot at them at the end If you're speaking to sell My own I'm only telling you me But for me, I can't get it done in less than 90 minutes Now I wound up at 75 on the success events and we paid a price for it I can't do it less than 90 and do it effectively for a product like magnetic marketing again If you go back through what we talked about today, there's certain things you have to know There's certain things you have to have agreed to in Sequence before you will buy that product if I have to cheat any of that You you really can't buy the product because we haven't had the sequential agreements I prefer two hours without a break Three to three and a half I'll stick a break in it because it allows me to create more than one selling break or a non-selling break and then a break So if you are speaking for other people by the way, you got a fight for what works for you So I just I got an engagement We only want to give you 90 minutes. No, I'm a bank said I got him two hours. I want two hours Screw somebody else I Don't care about them. I care about me. I want two hours. I got two hours If you're doing video Please think about some of these things one if you're doing it in studio Really think through whether you want to do green screen or whether you want to build sets nothing you do on green screen looks as good as a real set and And We'll get to it, but this is TV So very little is done on TV on green screens even today They have sets you can actually go to Hollywood and see them. There's a richness to it. There's a depth to it There's objects in it dressing a set in a way that symbolizes things and is meaningful to people and add some interest to What's going on is important locations lots of choices for locations besides the studio You might shoot in your house you might shoot in your office you can go to a factory to a place to a Hotel to an iconic site Right, so if you're doing a health pitch you can go stand in front of the the Mayo Clinic Until they catch you you can go I mean you can pick some iconic if you're doing a get rich thing You can go stand in front of the Statue of Liberty and you can talk about financial liberty I mean there's iconic sites that at least for a short time You can go film there quick and get out before anybody asks you about permits or any of that other stuff planes trains boats automobiles, you know, there's Seinfeld show now of comedians drinking coffee in cars or whatever and they sit in the car and talk to each other Drink coffee and people are tuning in and watching it on a webcast outdoors now outdoor shoots Add a lot of interest and they can allow you to make a point and to symbolize things I will tell you they are a giant pain in the butt And you got to allow some time and you got to have people who know what they're doing They are pretty painful most of the time. I shot an infomercial. You've never seen it and you won't But I shot an infomercial with Gary Halbert So many of you know Gary in Key West on-site What a horror show Of course, it's Key West. So, you know, no Member of the public is going to be cooperative with this at all. They are going to engage You know and a lot of people not the tourists but the people who live in Key West are pretty much there because they're not allowed to live Anywhere else This is like the last stop for them other than a prison That's who's there So and a number of them are pretty high and a number of them are pretty drunk all day long It's hot. It's humid. We shot one scene Halbert and the host who is Don Drysdale Don's dead now, but baseball player in Big High canvas chairs on the marina with the yachts, you know behind and Seagulls and shit. Well, and it's only I'm like this is like five minutes. I need I need two questions two answers That's all I need took me all day All right, just about the time you get it a seagull poops right on his head Gary goes He pooped on my head We finally I mean it is right I'm down to like three words has the yacht right behind Drysdale Somebody hits the button and disgorges the sewage Stuff's coming out got to start over you We're shooting How many of you knew Gary's significant other for many years your name is Paulette anybody know Paulette anybody come to Halbert seminars Dave you so Paulette love just a great woman, but not smart You know sort of intuitively smart, you know, but not just not smart So this scene is She drives up in front of the hotel and Gary's Rolls Royce gets out Walks to the hotel. This is not a speaking part. This is b-roll Drive up stop car get out walk to hotel It's all we need Right all day All day She pulls up in the wrong direction and gets out on the non hotel side And now where do I go? We're filming, you know, she pulls up gets out stands there Paulette, you know now we got a cue card says walk to hotel. This is what's going on I mean it took all day Drysdale by then was a pretty good drinker so He was only good until about noon Because the heat and his humidity and the booze in his body from the night before I was pretty good boozer myself And I mean you get a bunch of scotch in your body and go to sleep with it You were going to sweat it out the next day and you are less and less able to not look like you're drenched in sweat As the day goes on. I mean so outdoor stuff is just ugly but it adds an enormous amount of an interest to a Video and when you go to people's natural habitats and shoot there So you just got to decide how much pain, you know, you're willing to endure the live seminar stuff with audiences Works just make sure you got multiple cameras So you got stuff to cover and you shoot everybody laughing and everybody clapping And if you're going to shoot over a period of time You got to get them back in the seats They were in after they go to the bathroom Okay, because if they switch seats now you got problems. You can't let anybody leave early They got to be committed to be there because then you got empty seats. So you got to think through the audience thing Technical procedural stuff right and activity where they're going to watch you do something You've got to allow enough time to shoot that 56 different angles and ways and all of that filming. There's one camera shoots, which is what most people do for web stuff Two camera shoots three camera shoots for camera shoots. They're stationary and moving if you move while you Get recorded That sometimes you can't do it People they can't walk and talk at the same time. It's problematic for them Other people can do it really really well if you're filming an old person sometimes that's not good because they sort of shuffle and stuff A boom mic if you can afford it is a great thing to have a studio shoot because you get an extra moving Camera angle when you go into post production Extra shots to use in post biggest thing I'll tell you if you are shooting something that you are not just going to live Broadcast or you are not just going to slap up on the web as a one and done But you actually are shooting something that you want to live with for a while All your great work is going to be done in post Post production is where everything happens and you can't have enough footage So just about the time you think you've got it right shoot some more Get the boats with nobody sitting in front of them get the boat moving in get the boat moving out Get cover shot cover shot cover shot cover shot because he may not ever get the two sentences together the way we need him But he'll have all the pieces and if you don't have anything to cover him with While you continue to hear the sound you are screwed you're stuck with what's called a sharp cut you can see it I cut it and just slapped him together and took six words out. It's visible You there needs to be cover the boat and you got to be able to come back the other person That's why three camera shoots are better. You got one stationary wide shot I got a close-up on Ben and I got a close-up on him so for the whole time So I wind up with the ability to cover a couple of different ways Product beauty shots shoot the stuff you're selling Active somebody handling it with nice hands moving it around Stationary get a bunch of product beauty shots get a lot of b-roll get the product used if there's a way to use it If it's a notebook with pages in it get shots of somebody opening the notebook Looking through pages if they're gonna listen to CDs get some video of them taking the CD out of the package Putting it in the CD player. You won't know you want it necessarily until you get to post But you can't go back and get most of this If there's a celebrity if you rented a celebrity make sure you get every possible thing You could you would ever want from that celebrity now? Because you can't get them back So if you want them to handle the product you got to think through all of that if using a seminar or a studio audience You Can do as I said volunteers you can rent to order You do want to try and match the audience if they're going to be visible to Your your audience who's going to be buying And there's no point in having people in the visible audience who don't match up with your buyers So if you're selling weight loss to women, there's no reason to have a guy in the audience Stupid, right? I mean you got to know who your market is if you are candidly selling and 90% of your customers and 90% of the people you are driving to the webcast are white No reason to have you're not going to get the diversity award from your webcast No reason to have a black guy in an Asian and American Indian with a feather. Okay, there's no reason to have any of them Vice versa if a significant number of customers of yours are black you can't have all white folks in the audience They got to see themselves right if all your buyers are 55 and over and you got 20 year olds in there because You know your kid and three of his buddies were available to be in the audience bad idea So you want to match to your buyer get more than you need some of them won't show up Some of them which is really bad and you got to wedge them in the back But some of them will leave early even though they agreed not to leave early some of them will get sick All right, somebody will be in your audience and they'll be Scratching themselves the whole time and you're gonna have to get them out of there and replace them Clear and complete instructions before they come All right, and a babysitter Do not have an audience without one at least one person assigned to manage that audience They are not buicks that you park in position Put the parking gear on and let them idle. That's not how human beings are Have extra wardrobe on hand for them a Despite your instructions, they may show up He is going to show up in a purple polka dot Sweatshirt that says F you want And when you turn it inside out it says F you inside it You need to be able to dress that guy All right, the first thing he's going to do when he gets there is he's got a white shirt on and he's going to spill his coffee all over it So you got to have extra wardrobe make him happy audiences need to be happy So you got to make him happy the babysitter has to be able to interact with them You need to come out and engage them the presenter has to play well with the people The worst presenter in the world to put with a live audience is somebody that doesn't play well with the people So we're shooting a show. I won't name them Famous speaker you would know We're shooting a live audience show and we have stocked it all with his real testimonials at great difficulty So it's like 250 people in this audience It looks like the whole environment kind of looks like a TV like an Oprah show or something and he's on stage doing his thing and act to his Interaction with the audience and the host who's a celebrity is like Phil Donahue used to be so he's out in the audience With the microphone. This is what's going on And this woman stands up and starts telling her story. She's like Excited and crying and she burp. This is not choreographed. You couldn't choreograph it You couldn't pay enough she pushes herself past the celebrity She runs to the stage to give our guy a big hug and he does this You'll never get it again and the truth is he don't like anybody He don't like his customers and he don't like physical contact better not to make him interact with a live audience If you are dealing with inexperienced presenters Be very careful about doing cue cards or prompters They lock on them and you can tell they're locked on them You can tell they like follow the bobbing ball It's it's very hard very hard to disengage them and fix that you are far better off If you got an experienced host put him on prompter Okay, but just overshoot Just shoot a ton with them and clean it up in post be very careful about asking Amateurs to use pro tools you're better off letting them sit at the tall table on the stools with notes and Everybody sees they're dealing with notes then you are having them Tuned in to the prompter don't make demands They're not going to be able to do know what you got to deal with if you're shooting testimonials if you're shooting another Presenter limit the rehearsal if you're dealing with inexperienced presenters. They tend to get worse not better One run through and then take what you can get if you're using professional presenters then it's more about Real management of them and if they're hired if they're showing up to shoot you need time to befriend them So they like you little known secret about celebrities and I've treated very well And so they show up braced for a really bad experience with a jackass That's their attitude coming in the door and often their behavior will reflect it unless you have fixed that If Live to broadcast webcast and you're really serious about what you're doing and or there are Regulatory issues with what you're doing. There's why you may want to you may want tape delay just like in live television You do want live switch which means some of the editing is done as you go People who are good at it can do a lot of it if you're booking a studio talk to them about their live switch capabilities. I Said you want to do post and plan to get More than one use Out of whatever you do What if the first use doesn't work So when we took money from people produced infomercial I always strategize Let's make sure we get web video that you'll be able to use on the web Let's can we get a DV DVD out of this can we get five little videos? You'll be able to use what's all the different stuff we can get out of this day of production two days of production Whatever so if the main thing you did turns out to be a clinker you have other opportunities to do things with it We are out o'clock There are a couple of things we have not covered but there are a lot of notes about them So I think you will be fine and there is a bonus section of additional samples For you and your manual that I do not even have copies of And now I have lost my here it is So we are done After a half an hour break, huh? What page Help me tell me Oh the number one oh in a physical environment in a fit where you're selling in a room Yeah, the number one deadly never is never the academic auditorium Where the stage is at the bottom and the seats do this Okay, never never never never never never never never never Hey, it's all wrong you are diminished Secondly their theater seats they're in them now. They're comfortable. So your entire audience is looking down at you And you are like this unless you're on TV They are comfortable in theater seats. No good If you can sell in that environment you can sell on the top of a bus as it's driving across country Leaning over and yelling in the window. That's the right. So we are done. You've been a great group I hope you had a great day
